message:
fcp-oauth/token: only update expires_at when the refresh response carries expires_in

`OAuthTokens::update_from` unconditionally wrote the result of
`response.expires_in.map(|secs| now + Duration::seconds(secs))` into
`self.expires_at`. When the provider's refresh response omits
`expires_in` (which some providers do — they assume the original
token lifetime still applies and only issue new tokens), this
collapsed to `self.expires_at = None`, silently clearing the previous
expiry. Downstream `is_expired()` / `expires_soon()` checks would
then report the token as never-expiring, and the background refresh
loop would stop calling the token endpoint at all — eventually
leaving the session with a long-dead access token and no refresh
attempt in flight.

Fix: guard the `expires_at` update behind `if let Some(secs) =
response.expires_in { ... }`. When `expires_in` is absent, the
previous `expires_at` is preserved verbatim (together with the
existing per-field update rules for `access_token`, `token_type`,
`refresh_token`, `scope`, etc.).

The saturating arithmetic
(`i64::try_from(secs.min(u64::from(u32::MAX))).unwrap_or(i64::MAX)`)
is kept verbatim inside the guard: it prevents a malicious or buggy
provider from overflowing the `expires_at` into the past by
advertising a u64 duration bigger than i64::MAX / s.

`issued_at = now` remains unconditional, matching the existing
"this refresh happened now" semantics regardless of whether the
response restates the lifetime.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
